coredata.dc:title: A facile method to control the morphologies of barium sulfate particles by using carboxylic carbon quantum dots as a regulator
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lingyun
Yan, Ze
He, Xiu
Zhang, Xiaoqi
Wang, Suisui
Guo, Shiqian
Tang, Na
Wang, Xiaocong
coredata.dc:description: A very simple method to control the morphologies of BaSO4 particles was reported for the first time. Various morphologies of BaSO4 particles including rectangle, shuttle-like, flower-like and so on were prepared by using carboxylic quantum dots (CCQDs) as morphological regulators in presence of NaCl. The formation mechanism was deduced at the same time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: barium sulfate
carboxylic quantum dots
morphology control

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the controlling BaSO4 morphologies using CCQDs: (a) rectangular shape; (b) shuttle-like shape; (c) most of them remain shuttle-like shape, some of them changed to flower-like shape; (d) flower-like shape; (e) flower-like shape with shorter petals.
SEM imagines of BaSO4 particles obtained when the dosage of CCQDs was: (a) 0 mg/L; (b) 2 mg/L; (c) 4 mg/L; (d) 6 mg/L; (e) 8 mg/L; (f) 12 mg/L. Other conditions: T = 40 ℃; Time = 2 h; 2 g/L of NaCl.

SEM images of BaSO4 particles obtained at different dosage of NaCl (without CCQDs): (a) 1 g/L, (b) 4 g/L, (c) 8 g/L, (d) 20 g/L; and at different dosage of CCQDs (without NaCl): (a’) 2 mg/L, (b’) 4 mg/L, (c’) 8 mg/L, (d’) 12 mg/L.
SEM images of BaSO4 particles obtained at different dosage of NaCl with 2 mg/l CCQDs: (a) 1 g/L, (b) 4 g/L, (c) 8 g/L, (d) 16 g/L; and 6 mg/l CCQDs: (a’) 3 g/L, (b’) 6 g/L, (c’) 9 g/L, (d’) 18 g/L.

XRD patterns of BaSO4 particles with various morphologies obtained at different dosage of CCQDs: (a) 0 mg/L; (b) 2 mg/L; (c) 4 mg/L, (d) 6 mg/L; (e) 8 mg/L; (f) the standard PDF card (JCPDS No. 24-1035) of BaSO4.
Schematic growth mechanisms of BaSO4 particles when the dosage of CCQDs were: (a) 0 mg/L; (b) 2 mg/L; (c) 4 mg/L; (d) 6 mg/L.

head.ce:title: A facile method to control the morphologies of barium sulfate particles by using carboxylic carbon quantum dots as a regulator

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Material Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A very simple method to control the morphologies of BaSO4 particles was reported for the first time. Various morphologies of BaSO4 particles including rectangle, shuttle-like, flower-like and so on were prepared by using carboxylic quantum dots (CCQDs) as morphological regulators in presence of NaCl. The formation mechanism was deduced at the same time.
A very simple method to control morphologies of BaSO4 particles was proposed.
A new regulator of CCQDs was used to adjust the morphology of BaSO4.
Various shapes of BaSO4 particles were prepared.
The mechanism was also been proposed.

Schematic diagram of the controlling BaSO4 morphologies using CCQDs
ga1

ce:sections.ce:para: Barium sulfate (BaSO4), also be known as barite, is widely used because of its inertness, high specific gravity, opaqueness to X-rays and other properties [1]. It is one of the most important fillers used in the polymer materials, linoleum, rubber, lithographic ink, oil cloth and in pharmaceutical formulations and has been used extensively for investigating several phenomena of the precursors mixing, precipitation process, crystal growth and morphology [2–5].

Because the morphology of BaSO4 particles is critical for its properties and uses [6], the morphology control of BaSO4 particles has been the object of numerous studies [7,8]. In recent years, various surface modification agents have been utilized to tune the morphology of the BaSO4 particles, including organic acids such as octadecyl dihydrogen phosphate (n-C18H37OPO3H2, ODP) [9], stearic acid [10], ethylenediaminetetraa-cetic acid (EDTA) [11], and polymers such as polyacrylic acid, polyvinyl sulfonic acid and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [12]. However, many additives have some disadvantages, such as toxic, complicated in synthesis process, and can bring environmental pollution etc., so the BaSO4 particles modified by part of them are not suitable for biomedical uses and so on [13].

In this communication, a new regulator of carboxylic carbon quantum dots (CCQDs) was used to adjust the morphology of BaSO4. As a new class of carbon nanomaterials with size below 10 nm, CCQDs have received intensive attention due to its unique properties, such as low toxicity, high luminescent, robust chemical inertness, easy preparation and excellent solubility, making them promising in optoelectronic devices, sensors, bioimaging, etc. [14–16]. However, as far as we know, there is no report about using CCQDs to regulate the morphology of inorganic matters. In this paper, CCQDs were firstly used to adjust the morphology of BaSO4 and succeeded in synthesizing various morphological BaSO4 particles such as rectangular, shuttle-like, flower-like and so on. It is worth noting that these BaSO4 particles are stabilized. They can be directly used as pigments in various high-grade coatings or as fillers in plastics, rubber, ceramics and friction materials.

The CCQDs were derived from the pyrolysis of citric acid which has been described in our previous paper [17,18]. The decomposition temperature and time were 180 ℃ and 10 h respectively. The preparation process for various morphological BaSO4 particles was as follows: Firstly, 0.245 g of BaCl2•2 H2O were put into a baker and dissolved by 100 mL deionized water, and different amount of CCQDs (1, 2, 3, 4 and 6 mg) were added in; Then, about 1.0 g of NaCl and 0.5 mL of 1 M NaOH were added in, deionized water was added subsequently to make the volume of the solution is 250 mL; After that, 250 mL of Na2SO4 (0.142 g) solution was added in and the mixture was kept in a water bath at 40 °C for 4 h; Finally, the BaSO4 were collected through centrifugation and washed by deionized water for several times to completely remove the possible residual sodium (Na+) and chloride ions (Cl-).

A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100) image of the as-prepared CCQDs was shown in Fig. s1A. The synthesized CCQDs were about 5–8 nm in size, spherical in shape, and exhibited good dispersity. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy technology (FTIR, IS50) was used to explore the main functional groups contained in the products and the results were shown in Fig. s1B. From Fig. s1B, it could be found that the molecular characteristic spectra of citric acid disappeared and replaced by the characteristic spectra of CCQDs. More detailed evidences such as X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) about the rich carboxyl on the surface of the CCQD had been described in our previous paper [17].

Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram for the preparing different morphologies of BaSO4 particles. As shown in this diagram, various shapes of BaSO4 particles including rectangular, shuttle-like and flower-like could be controlled simply by adjusting the additive concentration of CCQDs. With the increase of the concentration of CCQDs, the morphologies of the prepared BaSO4 particles changed from simple to hierarchical structure.

Fig. 2 shows the scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSMIT-300LV) images of BaSO4 particles with different morphologies from using CCQDs as regulator in presence of NaCl. From Fig. 2, it could be observed the morphologies of BaSO4 particles changed significantly with the increasing of CCQDs dosage. When CCQDs were not added, the obtained BaSO4 was a rectangle sheet with an internal depression (Fig. 2a). With the addition of 2 mg/L CCQDs, the rectangular morphology changed to a shuttle-like shape with an internal convex (Fig. 2b). When the dosage of CCQDs increased to 4 mg/L, some flower-like crystals formed, some kept shuttle-like morphologies (Fig. 2c). Further increasing the CCQDs dosage to 6 mg/L, the particles were almost completely changed to flower-like morphologies (Fig. 2d). When the concentration of CCQDs was increased to 8 mg/L, the flower-like shape was still the main morphology, but the petals became shorter and granular petals appeared (Fig. 2e). The petals were further shortened to spherical granules when further increasing the dosage to 12 mg/L (Fig. 2f). If continued increasing the CCQDs concentration, BaSO4 crystal precipitation is difficult to obtain due to the high efficiency scale inhibition ability of CCQDs [17]. Therefore, we have not discussed the case of much higher concentration of CCQDs.

In order to investigate whether it was the CCQDs or NaCl, or the coordination of both, was the reason for the obvious changes of barium sulfate morphologies. Single-factor experiments without adding CCQDs ( Fig. 3a-d) and without adding NaCl (Fig. 3a’-d’) were performed respectively. From Fig. 3a-d we could observe that the morphology of BaSO4 particles remained rectangular shape with the increase of NaCl when without adding CCQDs. From Fig. 3a’-d’ we could find the BaSO4 particles kept polygonal-sheet-like shape with the increase of CCQDs when without adding NaCl. The above results indicated that the formation of various morphological BaSO4 particles was the synergistic effect of CCQDs and NaCl, rather than any single factor.

To further understand whether NaCl or CCQDs played a key role in the morphology controlling, the experiments of fixed CCQDs dosage and changing sodium chloride dosage was done. Fig. 4a-d and Fig. 4a’-d’ showed SEM images of BaSO4 particles obtained at different dosage of NaCl with 2 mg/L of CCQDs and 6 mg/L of CCQDs, respectively. From Fig. 4 a-d, we could find when the dosage of CCQDs was 2 mg/L, the morphology of BaSO4 kept the main shuttle-like shape as the concentration of NaCl increased from 1 g/L to 16 g/L. However, a lot of irregular particles could be found when the concentration of NaCl at a high level of 18 g/L (Fig. 4d). To obtain more data about different concentrations, we didn't use the same dosages of NaCl when investigating the effects of salt concentration on the dosage of 6 mg/L CCQDs. But the results were similar, when the dosage of CCQDs was 6 mg/L, the morphology of BaSO4 kept the main flower-like shape as the concentration of NaCl increased from 1 g/L to 18 g/L. It had little effect on the morphology at low concentration, and more irregular small particles appeared at high concentration. This result is reasonable, because there is a charge attraction between Ba2+ and Cl-, higher concentration of Cl- will affect the adsorption of Ba2+ to SO4 2-, thus affecting the ordered growth of BaSO4 crystal. Because Cl- is negatively charged like SO4 2-, they will form competitive adsorption to Ba2+ in this process. At the same time, there is a mutual repulsion between SO4 2- and Cl-. When the concentration of Cl- is low, this influence can be ignored because the interaction force between Ba2+ and SO4 2- is much higher than that of between Ba2+ and Cl-. However, the higher concentration of Cl- can reduce the relative number of SO4 2- around Ba2+. At the same time, the repulsion between SO4 2- and Cl- increases the difficulty of directional growth of BaSO4 crystals, resulting in the formation of more irregular shapes.

The above results revealed that the concentration of NaCl was not the key factor for the obvious change of BaSO4 morphology. In view of the high concentration of NaCl will lead to the increase of irregular particles, we selected a relatively low concentration of 2 g/L in the controlling synthesis process. Combined with the results of Fig. 2, we can conclude that the concentration of CCQDs is the decisive factor for the morphology change.

In order to further understand the mechanism of BaSO4 morphology change, an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, 6100, Cu Kα radiation: 1.5405 Å, 40 kV and 30 mA; scanning rate: 2°/min) was used to explore the crystal structure of typical samples with different morphologies. Fig. 5a-e showed the XRD patterns of BaSO4 obtained by fixing the addition of NaCl at 2 g/L and changing the dosage of CCQDs. Fig. 5f was the standard PDF card (JCPDS No.24–1035) of BaSO4. It could be observed that the crystal of BaSO4 didn’t change with the adding of CCQDs. But the crystallinity of BaSO4 particles decreased after adding CCQDs. The relative intensity of the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 19.9°, 26.9°, 31.6°, 32.7°, 42.8° and 44° also changed with the change of the CCQDs dosage, suggesting that the crystal had special orthorhombic growth in these directions [8].

The formation mechanism of various morphologies could be interpreted in terms of the micelle structure, electrostatic interactions between ions and micelles [19,20]. In the absence of CCQDs, barium ions (Ba2+) and sulfate ions (SO4 2-) were firstly forming BaSO4 nuclei, and the formed BaSO4 nuclei can preferentially adsorb one of the two kinds of ions. Due to the slightly excess of Ba2+, they were absorbed around the nuclei. And then, SO4 2- ions were adsorbed on the periphery of barium ions through electrostatic adsorption, further nucleated and grew up, eventually forming a thermodynamic stable rectangular structure (Fig. 2a). But when CCQD existed, the situation is changed. The surfaces of CCQDs were rich of carboxyl anions (-COO-) because there had an alkali treatment process before the use of CCQDs. The -COO- will chelate with Ba2+. So when sulfate ions were added in, SO4 2- and -COO- would compete to bind Ba2+. Therefore, when there were a small amount of CCQDs in the solution, due to the partial chelation of Ba2+, the nucleation probability of BaSO4 nucleus decreased gradually, thus forming shuttle-like shape (Fig. 2b). With the increase of CCQDs, the quantity of CCQDs adsorbed on the surface of barium sulfate increased, which made the growth of nuclei developed to several directions and forming flower-like shape (Fig. 2c-d). Further increasing the dosage of CCQDs, the nucleation growth probability of BaSO4 further reduced, the petals become shorter and even form globular granules (Fig. 2e-f). Because Na+ and Cl- ions were not directly involved in the direct nucleation process, they did not play a decisive role in the forming different morphology of BaSO4. However, due to the electrostatic attraction of the Ba2+ to Cl-, and the positively charged BaSO4 nuclei to Cl-, the presence of Cl- will affect the directional growth of BaSO4. Therefore, NaCl may be the main influence factor in the formation of various morphologies of BaSO4 crystals.

The schematic growth mechanisms of typical morphologies about BaSO4 particles were shown in Fig. 6. According to the above analysis and inference, it could be reasonably assumed that when CCQDs was absent, the BaSO4 crystals were arranged in parallel, forming the shape of rectangular sheet (Figs. 2a and 3a-d). When CCQDs were added in, the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CCQDs could bind Ba2+ on the surface of BaSO4 crystals, thus forming the morphology of shuttle-like shape as shown in the (Figs. 2b and 4a-d). With the increase of CCQDs, more and more CCQDs could bind to the surface of BaSO4 crystal, which made the growth of barium sulfate crystal in several directions and accumulated to flower-like shape (Figs. 2d-e and 4a’-d’). When the dosage of CCQDs was larger, they could bind more growth sites of BaSO4 crystals, so that more petals were formed, and finally the more irregular particles were found (Fig. 2e-f).

In summary, a very simple method to control morphologies of BaSO4 particles was proposed. Various morphologies of BaSO4 particles including rectangular, shuttle-like, flower-like and so on were prepared by adjusting the dosage of CCQDs in presence of 2 g/L NaCl, and this series of morphologies showed obvious gradual changes. When CCQDs were not added, the obtained BaSO4 was a rectangular shape. With the addition of 2 mg/L CCQDs, the rectangular morphology became a shuttle-like shape. Increasing the dosage to 4 mg/L, the flower-like shape appeared. When the dosage is 6 mg/L or more, almost completely flower-like shape was found. The mechanism of the gradual change was also proposed based on the principle of micelle structure formation. It must be emphasized that such BaSO4 particles are stabilized, which enables direct use as pigments and fillers or in ceramic processing and are promising candidates for materials science [21,22].

ce:section.ce:para: Lingyun Li: Methodology, Resources,Writing –originaldraft, Fundingacquisition. Ze Yan: Investigation,Validation,Formalanalysis,Datacuration. Xiu He: Formalanalysis,Investigation, Datacuration. Xiaoqi Zhang: Formalanalysis,Investigation. Suisui Wang: Formalanalysis,Validation. Shiqian Guo: Formalanalysis,Investigation. Na Tang: Conceptualization,Supervision, Writing–review&editing. Xiaocong Wang: Conceptualization, Writing–review&editing, Projectadministration.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the College Students’ Innovations Special Project Funded by Tianjin University of Science and Technology of China (No. 202010057177).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declared no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127668.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of nanoparticles by precipitation method using sodium hexa metaphosphate as a stabilizer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Gupta, P. Singh, C. Shivakumara, Synthesis of nanoparticles by precipitation method using sodium hexa metaphosphate as a stabilizer, Solid State Commun. 150 (2010) 386–388.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental and numerical investigation of mechanical and erosion behavior of barium sulphate filled glass fiber reinforced polymer composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Choudhary, A. Sharma, P. Agarwal, T. Singh, T. Patnaik, A. Patnaik, Experimental and numerical investigation of mechanical and erosion behavior of barium sulphate filled glass fiber reinforced polymer composites, Polym. Compos. 2021, 42, 753–773.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Kinetics of barium sulfate deposition and crystallization process in the flowing tube

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.T. Lu, K.Harouaka, S. Paudyal, S. Ko, M. Tomson, Kinetics of Barium Sulfate Deposition and Crystallization Process in the Flowing Tube, Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 59 (2020) 7299–7309.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication and surface properties of hydrophobic barium sulfate aggregates based on sodium cocoate modification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Hu, G. Wang, R. Cao, C. Yang, X. Chen, Fabrication and surface properties of hydrophobic barium sulfate aggregates based on sodium cocoate modification, Appl. Surf. Sci. 315 (2014) 184–189.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Isothermal crystallization kinetics and melting behavior of modified high-density polyethylene/barium sulfate nanocomposites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Nandakumar, P. Kurian, Chemosynthesis of monodispersed porous BaSO4 nano powder by polymeric template process and its characterisation, Powder Technol. 224 (2012) 51–56.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and application of CCQDs as a novel type of environmentally friendly scale inhibitor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.J. Vetter, Micelle structure in aqueous solutions of colloidal electrolytes. J. Phys. Colloid Chem. 51 (1947) 262–277.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical modeling of micelle growth. 4. Molecular thermodynamics of wormlike micelles from ionic surfactants: theory vs. experiment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.D. Danov, P.A. Kralchevsky, R.D. Stanimirova, S.D. Stoyanov, I.P. Stott, Analytical modeling of micelle growth. 4. Molecular thermodynamics of wormlike micelles from ionic surfactants: Theory vs. experiment, J. Colloid and Inter. Sci. 584 (2021) 561–581.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Formation of BaSO4 fibres with morphological complexity in aqueous polymer solutions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crystal design of barium sulfate using double-hydrophilic block copolymers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Qi, Helmut Cölfen, M. Antonietti, Crystal Design of Barium Sulfate using Double-Hydrophilic Block Copolymers, Angewandte Chemie. 39 (2010) 604–607.
